text: The surgical team sets up the instruments, attaching them to the five-millimeter allied medical ports. The camera used is a ten-millimeter, 30-degree down, 3D camera.

The robotic arm is being maneuvered into position through the port, demonstrating its versatility with wrist, shoulder, and elbow joints. The arm is held in a sword-like grip, contrasting with the pen-like grip of other robotic systems.

The internal view shows the setup for a middle lobectomy with a full range of instruments, including hooks, Marylands, bipolars, and a lung grasper. The third robotic arm is centrally positioned in the chest, with an anterior and a posterior port already inserted. The Maryland Grasper, a 5-millimeter instrument, is being inserted into the chest. The setup includes three robotic arms and an endoscopic camera.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to pull the middle lobe of the lung posteriorly, providing a clear view of the anterior hilum and the phrenic nerve. The tumor is visible, and dissection is initiated in this area.
The surgeon uses a hook diathermy to perform precise dissection, demonstrating skillful manipulation of the instrument within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Maryland instrument with bipolar energy on the left side to lift the middle lobe of the lung. A spatula and scissors with energy capabilities are available for use. Cigars are used to wipe away small areas of bleeding, with CO₂ insufflation maintained at six millimeters of mercury.

The surgeon exposes the middle lobe vein and proceeds to locate the middle lobe bronchus. A sizable lymph node is visible on the middle lobe bronchus, positioned anterior to the pulmonary artery. Using robotic assistance, the surgeon carefully dissects around the lymph node, utilizing two arms for precision while the third arm retracts tissue to reveal the junction between the middle lobe vein and bronchus.

The surgeon zooms in on the middle lobe bronchus, with a lymph node visible in front of it, obscuring the pulmonary artery and its branches. Using two Maryland forceps, the surgeon performs blunt dissection around the middle lobe vein, taking advantage of the delicate 5mm instruments for precise maneuvering.

The surgeon prepares to lift the middle lobe vein using a sling, facilitated by a low anterior 12mm port for the assistant. The procedure employs a 5mm CMR system and an 8mm Covidien curved tip stapler with 50 degrees of articulation, perfectly sized for the middle lobe vein.

The surgeon instructs the assistant to staple, while Sven uses bilateral blunt dissection around the middle lobe bronchus. The third arm lifts the bronchus, providing bimanual dexterity for precise dissection. A lymph node near the bronchus is visible, having been partially dissected.

The surgical team navigates around the middle lobe bronchus, utilizing a sling for better access. The camera provides a panoramic view, revealing the lower lobe, pericardium, and diaphragm. Ports are strategically placed, including a high midline port for the third arm, offering flexibility in positioning. The team identifies a prominent middle lobe pulmonary artery and decides to address it before proceeding with bronchus stapling.

The surgeon performs lymph node dissection using a Maryland bipolar instrument, applying energy to detach the lymph node completely. The node is then removed through the assistant port using a glove finger technique.

The surgeon prepares to address the bronchus, followed by the vein and one of the arteries. The robotic setup includes innovative port placements, with the third arm positioned at the midline, while the assistant port remains in a familiar location.

The surgeon successfully isolates the middle lobe bronchus using bipolar Maryland forceps, revealing a second segmental pulmonary artery. The instruments maneuver around the artery, which is visually similar in size to the vein, preparing for the final vessel firing. The view from below shows the delicate grasping technique used to manipulate the lung tissue without causing damage.

The surgeon carefully closes the jaws of the robotic instrument around the last segmental artery, ensuring the staple is not caught, and successfully staples the artery under direct vision. The robotic approach allows for a view from below, facilitating access to both anterior and posterior aspects of the surgical field, including station seven and the anterior hilum.
The surgeon uses a Covidien tri stapler with a 60 ml purple load to complete the fissure, finalizing the lobectomy. The surgical team positions the instruments carefully for optimal access.

The surgeon completes the middle lobectomy by firing a stapler across the minimal fissure, resulting in a clean separation of the lung tissue. The assistant uses scissors to finalize the lobectomy, ensuring all tissue stumps are neatly excised.
